_comment: Glossary source of truth. Each entry: term + definition. The build generates glossary.html with an anchor id = slugified term, matching the related_glossary links in chapters. Target is 500-700 terms; this is the comprehensive foundation covering every term the chapters reference plus core civics vocabulary.

terms.definition: Ratified in 1865, it abolished slavery throughout the United States.
Ratified in 1868, it granted citizenship to all persons born in the United States and guaranteed equal protection of the laws.
Ratified in 1870, it barred denying the right to vote based on race.
The movement to end slavery in the United States.
A formal change or addition to the Constitution.
A unit of the executive branch created to carry out specific government tasks.
In the World Wars, the nations — including the United States, Britain, and (in WWII) the Soviet Union — that fought against the opposing powers.
Dividing the 435 House seats among the states by population after each census.
Laws by which Congress sets aside money for specific government purposes.
One of the seven main sections of the Constitution.
The first national framework of U.S. government (1781), which created a weak central government and was replaced by the Constitution.
The head of the Department of Justice and chief lawyer of the federal government.
The list of candidates and questions a voter marks in an election.
Having two legislative chambers; Congress is bicameral, with a House and a Senate.
A proposed law under consideration by a legislature.
The first ten amendments to the Constitution, which protect individual liberties.
Fingerprints, a photograph, and a signature collected by USCIS during the naturalization process.
A 1773 protest in which colonists dumped British tea into Boston Harbor to oppose taxation.
The large system of departments, agencies, and career employees that carries out government work.
The group of department heads who advise the President and lead the executive departments.
One of the 15 major executive departments, each led by a Secretary (or, for Justice, the Attorney General).
The city where a government is seated; Washington, D.C. is the U.S. capital.
A meeting of party members to choose candidates or decide policy.
The count of the population taken every ten years, used to apportion House seats.
The official document proving that a person has become a U.S. citizen.
The system by which each branch of government can limit the powers of the others.
A full member of a nation, with its rights and responsibilities.
Taking active part in public life, such as voting, jury service, and community involvement.
The test of U.S. history and government given during the naturalization interview.
The 1964 law banning discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin.
The mid-20th-century effort to end racial discrimination and secure equal rights.
The body of career government employees hired for expertise who serve across administrations.
The 1861–1865 war between the Union and the Confederacy, caused chiefly by slavery.
The decades-long rivalry after World War II between the United States and the Soviet Union.
An elected lawmaking body in a colony — an early form of American self-government.
A territory settled and controlled by a distant country.
The President's role as head of the U.S. armed forces.
A small group of legislators that studies and shapes bills in a specific area.
A political and economic system that was the central U.S. concern during the Cold War.
An agreement in which each side gives up something; compromises were key to writing the Constitution.
Powers shared by the federal and state governments, such as the power to tax.
A loose union of states with a weak central government.
A temporary committee that reconciles differing House and Senate versions of a bill.
The Senate's approval of presidential appointments.
The legislative branch of the federal government, made up of the House and the Senate.
The geographic area represented by one member of the House.
A person represented by an elected official.
The supreme law of the United States, which establishes the government and protects rights.
The 1787 meeting in Philadelphia that wrote the Constitution.
The army of the colonies during the Revolution, led by George Washington.
The body of colonial delegates that governed during the Revolution and adopted the Declaration of Independence.
A naturalization interview outcome requiring more information or a re-test.
A regional unit of local government within a state.
A federal court that reviews decisions of the district courts.
The 1776 document announcing the colonies' separation from Great Britain.
A system of government in which power comes from the people, usually through voting.
A major division of the executive branch; see Cabinet Department.
The federal trial court where most federal cases begin.
The guarantee of fair legal procedures before the government takes a person's life, liberty, or property.
The process of choosing officials by vote.
A person who casts a state's vote for President in the Electoral College.
The group of electors who formally elect the President; 270 of 538 votes are needed to win.
The immigration station in New York Harbor through which millions of immigrants entered the United States.
Lincoln's 1863 order declaring enslaved people in the Confederate states to be free.
The government's power to take private property for public use, with fair payment.
The naturalization test of the ability to read, write, and speak basic English.
Powers specifically granted to the federal government by the Constitution.
The principle that each state has two senators regardless of its population.
The branch of government that carries out the laws, led by the President.
A directive from the President guiding how the executive branch carries out the law.
The early European voyages to and mapping of the Americas.
A body within the executive branch with a specific mission, such as USCIS or the IRS.
The division of power between the national government and the state governments.
A Senate tactic of extended debate used to delay or block a vote, usually ended by 60 votes.
Protects freedom of religion, speech, the press, assembly, and petition.
A vote taken by the full House or Senate on a bill.
The Application for Naturalization filed with USCIS.
The leaders who led the Revolution and founded the United States.
The election in which voters choose officeholders from among the parties' nominees.
Commander of the Continental Army and the first President of the United States.
Drawing district lines to favor one party or group.
The agreement creating a House based on population and a Senate with equal representation for each state.
The severe economic crisis that began in 1929.
The card showing that a person is a lawful permanent resident of the United States.
The right to be brought before a court to challenge unlawful detention.
The chamber of Congress with 435 members apportioned among the states by population.
The movement of people into a country to live there.
To bring formal charges against a federal official; the House has this power.
The process of charging and trying a federal official for serious misconduct.
The Senate proceeding that decides whether to remove an impeached official from office.
Fundamental rights — such as life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness — that cannot be taken away.
The ceremony in which a President is sworn into office.
July 4, marking the adoption of the Declaration of Independence in 1776.
The original inhabitants of the Americas, present long before Europeans arrived.
The shift to factory and machine production that transformed the economy.
The basic physical systems — roads, water, and power — that support society.
The letter USCIS sends scheduling a naturalization interview.
Harsh British laws of 1774 that punished Massachusetts and unified colonial resistance.
The branch of government that interprets the laws and the Constitution; the courts.
The power of courts to declare laws or government actions unconstitutional.
The authority of a government or court over a place or matter.
A citizen's responsibility to serve on a jury when called.
Serving on a jury to help decide a legal case; a responsibility of citizens.
An organization of workers that bargains for better wages and working conditions.
The branch of government that makes the laws; Congress.
Trying to influence the decisions of government officials.
City, county, and township governments, which are closest to daily life.
The 1803 purchase of vast territory from France that roughly doubled the size of the country.
The 19th-century belief that the United States was destined to expand across the continent.
The 1803 Supreme Court case that established the power of judicial review.
The federal health-insurance program for people 65 and older.
New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, and Delaware, known for farming and religious diversity.
A federal election held midway between presidential elections.
A city, town, or other unit of local government.
"The Star-Spangled Banner," the official song of the United States.
State-based military forces that can be called up by a governor or the President.
The legal process by which a non-citizen becomes a U.S. citizen.
President Franklin D. Roosevelt's programs to fight the Great Depression.
Massachusetts, New Hampshire, Rhode Island, and Connecticut.
States that people retain rights beyond those specifically listed in the Constitution.
The colonial principle that taxes require the consent of elected representatives.
The pledge taken to become a U.S. citizen, promising loyalty to the Constitution.
Congress's power to enact a bill over a presidential veto by a two-thirds vote in each chamber.
Congress's monitoring of the executive branch through hearings and investigations.
The President's power to forgive a federal crime.
The 1941 Japanese attack that brought the United States into World War II.
A non-citizen authorized to live permanently in the United States; a green card holder.
English settlers who founded Plymouth Colony in 1620 seeking religious freedom.
A large farm, often worked by enslaved people, common in the Southern colonies.
An organized group that seeks to win elections and shape government policy.
The principle that the power of government comes from the people.
The total number of votes cast directly by the people.
The introduction to the Constitution, which states its purposes.
A past court decision that guides future cases.
The head of the executive branch and head of state of the United States.
An election in which a party's nominee for an office is chosen.
An early-1900s period of reform in business, labor, and government.
Schooling provided and funded by government.
Government services such as police and fire protection.
Services provided by government, such as roads, water, and mail.
The minimum number of members needed for a legislature to conduct business.
Official approval, as of the Constitution or an amendment.
The post–Civil War effort (1865–1877) to rebuild the South and protect the rights of freed people.
A detailed rule issued by an agency to carry out a law.
Acceptance of different religious beliefs, notably practiced in colonial Pennsylvania.
A system in which citizens elect officials to make decisions on their behalf.
A government in which power rests with the people and their elected representatives.
Powers kept by the states under the Tenth Amendment.
A bill to raise money through taxes, which must begin in the House.
The principle that everyone, including leaders, must obey the law.
The 1777 American victory that convinced France to join the Revolution.
The elected local body that governs public schools.
Government searching or taking property; limited by the Fourth Amendment.
The withdrawal of Southern states from the Union before the Civil War.
The head of an executive department (except the Department of Justice, led by the Attorney General).
Loyalty to one's region, especially the North–South divide over slavery.
The enforced separation of people by race.
People governing themselves through elected representatives.
The chamber of Congress with two members from each state.
The Senate's approval of presidential nominees such as Cabinet officers and judges.
Dividing government into legislative, executive, and judicial branches.
Established the direct election of U.S. senators by the voters (1913).
A 1786–1787 uprising that exposed the weakness of the Articles of Confederation.
The federal program providing retirement and disability benefits.
The federal agency that runs the Social Security program.
Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, and Georgia, which had plantation economies.
The leader of the House of Representatives, second in line to the presidency.
The arrangement by which only about one-third of senators are elected every two years.
A 1765 British tax on printed materials that sparked colonial protest.
A monument in New York Harbor symbolizing freedom and welcoming immigrants.
The right to vote.
The clause making the Constitution and federal law the highest law of the land.
The highest court in the United States.
The highest law, which all other laws must follow; in the U.S., the Constitution.
Reserves to the states or the people the powers not given to the federal government.
A legal limit on how long an official may serve; the President is limited to two terms.
The legislative, executive, and judicial divisions of government.
A direct form of local self-government common in New England.
The forced removal of the Cherokee and other nations westward, with great loss of life.
A formal agreement between nations; treaties require Senate approval.
The 1783 treaty that ended the Revolutionary War and recognized U.S. independence.
Limits the President to two terms in office (ratified 1951).
A political system dominated by two major parties.
Cruel and oppressive government; what the separation of powers guards against.
A federal document that proves citizenship and allows international travel.
The growth of cities as people move from rural areas.
The harsh 1777–1778 winter encampment that tested the Continental Army.
The President's rejection of a bill passed by Congress.
Official records of births, marriages, and deaths kept by state and local governments.
Signing up to vote before an election, as required by most states.
The 1965 law protecting the right to vote, especially for Black Americans.
The 1914–1918 global war that the United States entered in 1917.
The 1939–1945 global war that the United States entered after Pearl Harbor in 1941.
The 1781 battle where the British surrender effectively ended the Revolutionary War.
The economic system of the United States, in which businesses and individuals — not the government — own most property and decide what to produce and sell. Also called a free market economy.
An economy in which prices and production are set mostly by supply and demand rather than by the government; another name for U.S. capitalism.
The forces that guide prices and production in a free market: scarce, wanted goods cost more, while plentiful or less-wanted goods cost less.
The central bank of the United States, which manages the money supply and interest rates to keep the economy stable.
A tax on earnings, authorized for the federal government by the 16th Amendment, that helps fund the federal budget.
The federal government's plan for raising and spending money, funded largely by taxes.
Essays by James Madison, Alexander Hamilton, and John Jay (as 'Publius') that explained and defended the Constitution to win its ratification.
The system requiring most men ages 18 through 25 to register so a military draft could be organized fairly if ever needed.
